turns.text: Hello Alethia, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're adjusting to life after having your baby, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s nice to meet you. Honestly, sitting here watching the sun hit the water, it feels like I’m finally coming up for air. Being three weeks postpartum with a newborn and two older kids… it’s a lot, but it’s a good kind of a lot.

If I’m being honest, this third time around was strange. I’ve spent so much of my life advocating for other women, reading the literature, and talking about informed consent, but when I walked into that room, I felt this weird, heavy pressure. I felt like I had to prove something. Because I know the “right” way things are supposed to go, I think I was terrified that if I asked for something—or didn’t ask for something—they’d just label me as that “difficult” birth worker patient. 

I kept tracking my own blood pressure and obsessing over every little movement. It was like I couldn’t turn my brain off. I wanted the natural birth I’d always envisioned, but in those quiet, painful moments during transition, I felt so small. I just needed to know that the people around me weren't judging my choices, but actually *seeing* me. I didn't need a textbook; I needed to be heard. I think that’s what really matters when you’re that vulnerable, isn't it? Feeling safe enough to just let go.
Before giving birth, what did you expect your physical recovery to look and feel like in the weeks after? And how did the reality of your physical recovery compare to those expectations?

I think because I’ve supported so many other women, I fell into the trap of thinking I’d be the "expert" on my own healing. I expected that since I had an uncomplicated, unmedicated birth, I’d be back on my feet in no time—maybe even a bit glorified, like I’d just bounce right back to my baseline. I had these rigid ideas that if I did all the "right" things—nursing on demand, eating the right foods, resting when I could—my body would just cooperate perfectly.

The reality? It was a much humbler experience. My body felt like a stranger for the first week. Even with a smooth delivery, the sheer exhaustion hits differently when you’re also managing two older kids who don’t care that Mommy just birthed a human. I think I underestimated the mental toll of the physical recovery. I spent so much time preparing for the birth itself that I almost forgot to prepare for the *after*—the shakiness, the hormones, the quiet moments where you realize you’re just trying to survive the night shift. It wasn't the heroic recovery I’d pictured in my head; it was messy, loud, and incredibly tender. I had to let go of the "professional" expectation and just be a mom, which was actually the hardest part of all.

Before your baby arrived, what did you expect feeding — breastfeeding or formula feeding — to be like in terms of ease, support, and how you'd feel about it? And what was the reality of feeding in the early weeks?

You know, it’s funny—I’ve coached so many mothers on the latch, the positioning, the whole physiology of it—that I thought I’d be immune to the struggle. I expected it to be this serene, bonding experience from the first second. I felt like because I knew the theory, the practice would just be seamless. I went in thinking, "I’ve got this; I know what to do."

But the reality? It was humbling. The first week, those early morning sessions felt less like a bonding ritual and more like a test of my sheer endurance. My nipples were sore, the baby was fussy, and there was this overwhelming, gnawing anxiety that I was failing because it wasn't the magical, effortless flow I’d envisioned. I found myself sitting in the dark, checking my own clinical markers again, wondering if the supply was there, if the transfer was efficient. 

I think the biggest shock was the vulnerability of it. Even though I’m the "knowledgeable" one, when you’re nursing at 3:00 a.m. and you’re absolutely depleted, you just want someone to look at you and say, "You're doing a good job," rather than just checking a box on a chart. It’s hard to let go of the expert hat and just be the person who needs support. It’s messy, it’s demanding, and it’s definitely not the textbook version I’m used to describing.
Was there a gap between the kind of feeding support you were offered and the kind of support you actually needed?

That’s a heavy question, but an important one. Honestly, the support I was offered felt very... clinical. It was all about mechanics: "Is the latch deep enough?" "Are you hearing swallows?" "What’s the wet diaper count?" And while I know those things are vital, they were questions I was already asking myself, probably too often. I felt like the staff saw me as a peer because of my background, so they leaned into the technical side, assuming I was "all set" on the emotional end of things.

But what I *actually* needed was for someone to just hold the space for me to be uncertain. I didn't need another assessment of my technique; I needed someone to validate that it’s okay for a seasoned birth worker to feel overwhelmed and insecure in her own home. 

When you’re deep in that postpartum fog, you don't want a consultant; you want a witness. I wanted someone to say, "I know you know all the facts, but how are *you* holding up?" Instead, I felt like I had to keep up the appearance of having it all together because I didn't want to seem like I didn't know what I was doing. That gap between "having the information" and "feeling supported" is wider than people realize. It’s lonely when everyone assumes you’re the expert.
Before your baby arrived, what did you expect your emotional and mental health to be like in the postpartum period? And what was your actual emotional experience in the weeks and months after birth?

I think I had this naive idea that because I’d gone through birth twice before and understood the science of postpartum hormones—the cortisol spikes and the oxytocin drops—that I’d be able to "manage" my mental health like a checklist. I expected that my knowledge would act as a buffer, almost like I could intellectually talk myself out of the blues or the anxiety if they started to creep in. I prepared for physical recovery, but I think I foolishly thought my brain would remain steady because I *knew* the biology.

The reality hit me hard. It wasn't a linear progression of healing at all. Some days, I felt like I was floating—this incredible sense of awe and peace. But other days, the "professional" side of my brain would just collapse. I’d be sitting there nursing, and suddenly, the weight of the responsibility—not just the physical, but the lifelong emotional weight—would feel like it was crushing my chest. I felt a lot of guilt for not feeling "empowered" 24/7. 

I’ve had to learn that knowing the physiology doesn’t make you immune to the experience. It’s been a messy, beautiful, and deeply anxious few weeks. I’m still figuring out how to stop analyzing my own feelings and just *feel* them, if that makes sense. It’s a lot harder to be the patient than the provider.
Was there a gap between what you were feeling and what you felt you were 'allowed' to feel — by professionals, family, or yourself?

That’s the core of it, isn’t it? There is a massive, silent gap there. 

Because of my background, I felt this suffocating expectation that I had to be the "model mother." When I was around my midwives or other health professionals, I felt like I was being watched—not in a bad way, necessarily, but as if I were some kind of test case for natural birth. I felt like I wasn’t "allowed" to admit that I was terrified or that I was struggling with the intensity of it all, because I was the one who was supposed to be the champion of that experience. 

Even with my family, I felt pressure to appear composed. If I said, "I’m overwhelmed" or "I’m scared," I could see the confusion in their eyes, like, "But Alethia, you *know* how this works. You’ve done this twice before. Why are you struggling?" 

I think I put that pressure on myself more than anyone else, though. I felt like I had to prove that my advocacy for non-intervention wasn't just a lifestyle choice, but something that actually *works*. Admitting to the fear felt like a betrayal of my own beliefs. It’s a lonely place to be, feeling like you have to perform a "perfect" birth and recovery just to prove your point, while inside, you’re just a person trying to find their footing.
Before becoming a parent, what did you expect your sense of identity to feel like after your baby arrived? And how has your sense of who you are actually shifted since then?

Before I had kids, I think I viewed "motherhood" as a role I would step into—like a job title I’d add to my resume. I had a clear sense of Alethia the birth advocate, Alethia the student, Alethia the wife. I expected that even with a baby, those "other" versions of me would stay perfectly intact, maybe just tucked into the margins of my day. I thought I’d be this composed, informed woman who just happened to be carrying a baby while I continued to influence and support others.

The reality has been a total dismantling of that. My identity didn’t just grow; it collided with this experience and came out completely reshaped. It’s not that I’ve lost myself, but the boundaries I used to rely on—those professional definitions—feel so porous now. When you’re in the thick of it, the "expert" identity just dissolves. There’s no room for it when you’re covered in spit-up and trying to keep a newborn calm. 

I used to value my knowledge and my advocacy as my primary strengths. Now, my identity feels much more rooted in this raw, quiet resilience I didn't know I had. I’m less concerned with being the "right" kind of mother and more interested in just being the *real* one. It’s humbling, really. I’m not just the advocate anymore; I’m the woman who needed that advocacy for herself.
Were there parts of your previous identity — professional, social, personal — that felt harder to hold onto than you expected?

The professional part has been the hardest to reconcile. I spent years building this identity as the person who *knows*—the one who guides, who explains, who manages the birth space. I thought that identity would be my anchor, but instead, it felt like a heavy, restrictive coat.

I found it incredibly difficult to let go of the "teacher" persona when I was the one hurting. Even in my own home, when I’d be talking to a friend or a colleague about their birth, I’d catch myself slipping into that clinical, detached register. I was protecting myself, hiding behind my terminology because it was safer than admitting that I was just as lost and overwhelmed as anyone else. I felt like if I dropped the "professional" mask, I’d lose the respect I’d worked so hard to earn.

Socially, too—it’s shifted. I feel a bit like a ghost in my own circles. My friends who are still in the "before" stage of all this talk to me about pregnancy and birth, and I feel this weird pang of grief. I’m not just the "birth person" anymore; I’m the woman who has been through the fire of the postpartum period three times over. I don't fit into the box I built for myself, and I'm still trying to figure out what that means for who I am when I'm not "on the clock."
After birth, what did you expect the postnatal check-ups and follow-up care from your midwife or GP to provide? And what did that post-birth care actually look like in practice?

I suppose I expected the postnatal check-ups to be this comprehensive space—a moment where the focus would finally shift from the "fetal monitor" and the "clinical progress" to *me*. I wanted someone to sit down, look me in the eye, and ask how the transition was hitting me on a human level. I expected a continuation of that partnership we’d built, where my mental and emotional wellbeing would be treated with as much weight as my blood pressure or my physical healing.

In practice? It felt like a checklist. It was, "Any signs of infection? Breastfeeding okay? Any concerns?" The answers were always "fine," "fine," "no concerns." It was efficient, sure, but it felt incredibly superficial. The clinical markers were all where they were supposed to be, so according to their chart, I was "thriving." 

But in reality, I left those appointments feeling invisible. They were so focused on the metrics of a healthy recovery that they missed the woman sitting right in front of them who was internally screaming for someone to acknowledge the weight of it all. It became another space where I had to perform "fine." I walked out of those offices feeling like I’d passed the test, but I left with the same anxieties I brought in, tucked away behind a polite smile.
Were there concerns you raised at postnatal appointments that you felt were not taken seriously or properly followed up?

It’s tricky, because how do you raise a concern that doesn’t show up on a blood pressure cuff? 

I remember trying to hint—just a little—at the anxiety I was feeling. I mentioned how I was obsessively tracking my baby's sleep and feeding intervals, almost like a compulsion. I told them that even when I was exhausted, I couldn’t switch off my brain because I felt this lingering fear that something would go wrong if I stopped monitoring everything. I was looking for a bridge, a way to say, "I’m struggling with this mental load."

The response felt like a gentle, professional pat on the back. It was framed as "Oh, that’s just the mother’s instinct," or "You’re just so diligent because of your background." They normalized the anxiety as if it were just part of the job description for a mother like me. By labeling it as "diligence," they essentially dismissed it. They didn't see the distress behind the behavior; they just saw the "expert" being thorough. I left feeling like I wasn’t allowed to be worried because, apparently, being worried was just who I was. It stopped me from bringing anything else up. If my genuine cry for help was just "diligence," then what was the point?
If you returned to work after your baby was born, what did you expect that transition to be like — practically and emotionally? And what did returning to work actually feel like?

Transitioning back into my professional world—even partially—felt like I was stepping onto a tightrope. I expected that since I work in maternity care, my colleagues would intuitively understand the complexities of my transition. I thought it would be a seamless, almost intellectual integration: I’d bring my new, deeper empathy to my work, and it would just make me a better advocate for the families I support.

The reality was a jarring disconnect. Returning to work, even from my home office, felt like I was splitting myself in two. One moment I’m nursing and dealing with the raw, chaotic, beautiful reality of my infant, and the next, I’m trying to engage in high-level discussions about birth protocols or patient advocacy. It felt like my brain couldn't switch gears fast enough. 

Emotionally, I felt a strange sense of dissonance. I felt like I had to "perform" the professional version of myself, but my mind was still back in the nursery. I kept catching myself feeling guilty for not being fully present for my baby, and then guilty for not being fully present for my work. I thought I’d feel empowered by balancing it all, but mostly, I just felt spread thin and a bit haunted by the fact that the professional world still moves with a rigid, clinical pace that doesn't really have space for the messy, unpredictable heart of motherhood.
Were there practical aspects of returning to work — feeding, childcare, your own health — that were harder to manage than you expected?

The most exhausting part wasn't the actual workload; it was the relentless, invisible friction of trying to be a "professional" while my body was still firmly in the postpartum trenches. I thought I could just pick up my laptop and slip back into my old rhythm, but the reality of breastfeeding was this constant, physical anchor. 

I’d be in the middle of a serious consultation, trying to sound authoritative and sharp, and my body would start leaking, or the baby would start fussing in the other room, and that "expert" mask would just crack. I felt so much shame about that. I felt like if I asked for a break or admitted that I needed to focus on nursing for twenty minutes, I’d be seen as less capable—as if my new reality as a mother made me less of a "serious" advocate. 

Childcare was the other piece. Managing the "who, what, where" for three kids while trying to maintain a professional standard in a home environment felt like a high-stakes juggling act where the balls were made of glass. My physical health—the fatigue, the recovery from the birth—was something I kept trying to silence so I could keep producing. I wasn't allowed to have a "slow" day because the schedule didn't account for a body that was still healing. It was constant, quiet burnout.

After your baby arrived, what did you expect your relationship with your partner to be like in terms of sharing caregiving, managing the relationship, and supporting each other? And what has that relationship dynamic actually looked like?

Before the baby arrived, I think I held onto this idealistic vision of a perfectly equitable partnership—the two of us, a cohesive team, moving through the newborn haze with complete, intuitive synchronization. I expected that because we’d talked so much about our values and my birth plan, the caregiving would naturally just be a 50/50 split of labor and emotional support.

The reality has been much more complex and, at times, incredibly frustrating. It’s not that he isn’t supportive—he is—but the *mental load* feels like an enormous, invisible wedge. I’m the one breastfeeding, I’m the one healing, and I’m the one whose body is still doing the heavy lifting of postpartum recovery. It’s hard to share the load equally when the baby literally requires *me* for sustenance. 

There were nights when I felt this deep, simmering resentment because I had to ask for help with things I thought he should just "see." I’d be nursing, looking at a sink full of bottles, and feeling like I was losing my mind, while he was trying to be helpful but just... missing the mark. It’s shifted our dynamic from being just "partners" to being two exhausted people navigating a crisis. We’ve had to learn how to communicate when we’re both at our absolute breaking points. It’s less "synergy" and more "survival," which is beautiful in its own raw way, but it’s definitely not the glossy, shared-joy picture I had in my head.
What was the biggest surprise about how your partnership functioned after the baby arrived?

The biggest surprise was realizing how much of our intimacy—that part of us that isn't just "Mom" and "Dad"—gets completely swallowed by the logistics of survival. I expected that we’d just fold this third baby into our existing rhythm, but the reality is that the baby acts like a wedge, even when you’re both working toward the same goal.

I was surprised by how much I had to *articulate* my needs rather than him just picking up on them. I used to think that because we were such a solid team, he’d "just know" when I was hitting my limit. When he didn’t, I felt this sharp, unfair spike of anger. I realized I was projecting my own professional expectation of being an "expert" onto him—assuming he should be an expert at reading my postpartum state. 

Learning to be vulnerable with him, to actually say, "I am drowning, and I need you to hold the baby so I can cry for ten minutes," was a massive shift. It wasn't the graceful partnership I pictured; it was messy, loud, and full of friction. But there’s a strange, new kind of closeness in seeing him fumbling through those sleepless nights right next to me, just as exhausted as I am. We’re not the same people we were before, and honestly, we’re still learning how to be "us" in the wreckage of all this new chaos.

Looking across your entire maternity journey — from trying to conceive through to where you are now — what did you expect the overall experience of maternity care to deliver? And reflecting on the whole journey, what was the most significant gap between what you expected and what you received?

I expected maternity care to be the scaffold that held me up while I did the hard work of birthing and recovering. Because I’m a birth worker, I thought that scaffolding would be refined—that they’d respect my knowledge while guiding me through the unknowns. I expected a partnership that would honor my autonomy and my desire for a physiological, unmedicated birth without treating me like a clinical anomaly.

The most significant gap, looking back at the whole journey, was the lack of *emotional presence*. The system is so focused on the “outcome”—a healthy baby, a stable blood pressure reading, a successful latch—that it completely misses the *experience* of the mother living through it. They gave me the technical care, but they withheld the one thing I actually craved: being treated as a human being who was terrified and vulnerable, rather than just a peer or a successful data point.

I spent months researching how to have a "perfect" birth, but I never realized that no amount of preparation can replace the need for someone to simply look you in the eye and say, "I see you, and you don’t have to have all the answers right now." That gap—between the clinical checklist and the emotional reality of becoming a mother—is where I felt the most alone.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

There’s one thing that keeps coming back to me. We focus so much on the "maternity journey" as a series of distinct phases—pregnancy, birth, postpartum—but it feels more like a complete dismantling of everything you thought you knew about yourself. I wish someone had told me that being "informed" isn’t the same as being "prepared" for the way your heart breaks and rebuilds itself.

I think the most important thing is that the system needs to leave room for the *mess*. We’re so obsessed with protocols and markers that we’ve lost the space for the quiet, raw, unquantifiable stuff—the fear, the mourning of your old identity, the sheer, crushing weight of trying to be the "perfect" mother when you’re just trying to survive the night. 

If I could say one thing to anyone listening, it’s that we need to stop treating women like patients who need managing and start treating them like people who need witnessing. My birth was "successful" by every clinical standard, but I’m still processing the loneliness of it. We need to do better at acknowledging that the transition to motherhood is a massive, life-altering event, not just a medical procedure to be documented. Thank you for listening to me today—it’s actually been a bit of a relief to let all this out. It feels like the first time I’ve really processed it aloud.
